interactions.summary: Jiang explains why he made predictions publicly, why elite friends missed obvious events, and how Yale trauma taught him a false meritocracy/IQ/zero-sum/failure worldview he later had to overcome.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Interview exchange: SPEAKER_00 is host Jack Neel or ad read; SPEAKER_01 is Jiang unless noted.

speaker_notes.note: Diarization is consistent enough for this packet: SPEAKER_00 carries host questions, intro, outro, or sponsor reads; SPEAKER_01 carries Jiang answers. Sponsor ad segments are not Jiang claims.

claims.claim: Jiang says he posted predictions publicly because he truly believed them and wanted a historical record that could prove or disprove him.
Jiang says Yale did not teach him power; as a poor alienated student, he dissociated and left with a naive meritocratic, IQ-centered, zero-sum, failure-terrified worldview.

signature_moments.moment: Obvious reality could not pass through the skulls of Yale and Harvard friends.
signature_moments.source_phrase: impossible to relay obvious reality to them
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why he turned private arguments into public prediction.

signature_moments.moment: Yale alienation made him miss power rather than decode it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I wasn't actually observing how power works
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It reverses the host's assumption about elite education as insight.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's former belief that the best ideas and best work would naturally win without friends, money, or connections.

glossary_terms.term: Three Things Yale Didn't Teach Me
glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's article naming IQ worship, zero-sum competition, and fear of failure as educational/psychological problems.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang locates the study-abroad program crisis in 2008 and the YouTube prediction turn around the COVID era.
